summary: Gruber reports on Apple's loss in a landmark U.K. lawsuit where the Competition Appeal Tribunal ruled Apple abused its dominant position by charging unfair App Store commissions from 2015 to 2020, potentially costing Apple up to £1.5 billion. Rather than focusing on the specifics of this ruling, Gruber argues that the significance lies in its consistency with every other regulatory and antitrust action worldwide — Apple is losing everywhere. He contends that Apple's strategy of insisting it is right and regulators are wrong is failing, regardless of the merits. Gruber urges Apple to proactively reform the App Store's rules on its own terms rather than waiting for bureaucrat-designed concessions to be forced upon them. He frames this as a strategic imperative: how Apple thinks things should work is not how they are working out. He references his own years-old argument that the best time for Apple to adjust was over a decade ago.
key_points: The U.K. ruling found Apple abused its dominant position through excessive App Store commissions from 2015-2020, potentially costing hundreds of millions of pounds
The ruling's significance is not its novelty but its consistency with every other global regulatory action against App Store practices — Apple cannot point to a single recent win
Apple's strategic posture of insisting it is right and regulators are wrong is failing regardless of whether Apple is substantively correct
Gruber argues Apple should proactively offer its own concessions on distribution exclusivity, IAP exclusivity, and commission rates rather than have worse terms imposed by regulators
The best time to reform was over a decade ago; the next best time is now
money_quotes: When is the last time an investigation regarding the legality of the App Store's dominant market position went in Apple's favor, in any country? I can't recall one.
Apple is clearly fighting a losing battle here.
Whether Apple ought to be losing all these legal and regulatory battles regarding the App Store is, from a strategic standpoint, almost irrelevant. The obvious fact is, they are losing them.
Apple has approached all this regulatory conflict from a perspective that they're right, and the regulators are wrong.

At some point, after being handed loss after loss in rulings from courts and regulatory bodies around the globe, shouldn't they change their strategy and start trying to offer their own concessions, rather than wait for bureaucrat-designed concessions to be forced upon them?
However Apple thinks all of this should work out is not the way it is working out.
The best time to adjust the rules of the App Store was over a decade ago. The next best time to make those adjustments is now.
key_insight: Apple's insistence that it is right about the App Store is strategically irrelevant when it is losing every legal and regulatory battle worldwide, and it should proactively reform before worse terms are imposed.
